results.query: Where was Lindy born and what were the circumstances of his early life?
results.context:
[Source: memoirs_main, Time: 0:37]
and go back and start relating the story from where I was born at Remsen, Iowa, October the 7th, 1902. My folks were living on the farm, as far as I know. They were farming at that time. My dad had been a drayman and a policeman in a town of 1,200 with 11 barrooms in it. Those were the hectic days, the pioneer days for western Iowa. However, he farmed for some years. He had three or four teenage boys at that time, so he thought it best to get out on the farm. And for some reason or other, some land agent got a hold of him and suggested that with a gang of boys like he had, he should be up in Canada where land was cheap and a lot of room for expansion. At that particular time, land in Iowa was selling, well, roughly $80 an acre, where you could buy it up in Canada from $20 to $25 an acre. But it was still the raw prairie. It had to be broke up and put into cultivation before you could get anything off it. And I guess they did a good job selling because dad had a lot of courage to move up in that godforsaken country when he was in a good corn growing,

---

[Source: memoirs_main, Time: 2:08]
well-established community down there. Well, we went up to Canada and they had about three boxcars of, I guess, mostly animals and some machinery. They didn't have much machinery that you could use up in Canada. But along with what they took along, they had a boxcar load of it. And the rest was packed up in the passenger train and headed out. I don't know the history of whether we—I'm sure we didn't have sleepers. And we would have had to stop possibly overnight in Minneapolis when we got there. I don't remember any of that. I do faintly remember some of the train ride. And there were—the two boys went with the boxcars of animals. And they tell of all the fun they had riding in those side door pullmans, if they call them, along with the animals, all the way up from Remsen, Iowa, up to Sioux Falls, Sioux City, and then up what they call the Sioux Line, up to Halbrite. And landed there on the 17th of March, after a winter of the greatest snowfall the prairie had ever seen. There was an estimated 36 inches of snow in Malapa that winter. However, we landed at Halbrite. And now this is where my memory starts to get in gear.

---

[Source: memoirs_main, Time: 3:41]
We stayed in the hotel. And I have a faint recollection of us being in this hotel. This is Mother and all the children. And then the boys and Dad arrived up there. Well, I guess Dad was on the train with us. And the boys came up with the livestock, and they had to unload. And take it out to the farm. Now, when they got out there, everything was under snow. They tell that they had to dig into a snowbank to find the barn, a stable.

[Source: memoirs_main, Time: 1:21]
And for some reason or other, some land agent got a hold of him and suggested that with a gang of boys like he had, he should be up in Canada where land was cheap and a lot of room for expansion. At that particular time, land in Iowa was selling, well, roughly $80 an acre, where you could buy it up in Canada from $20 to $25 an acre. But it was still the raw prairie. It had to be broke up and put into cultivation before you could get anything off it. And I guess they did a good job selling because dad had a lot of courage to move up in that godforsaken country when he was in a good corn growing, well-established community down there. Well, we went up to Canada and they had about three boxcars of, I guess, mostly animals and some machinery. They didn't have much machinery that you could use up in Canada. But along with what they took along, they had a boxcar load of it. And the rest was packed up in the passenger train and headed out. I don't know the history of whether we—I'm sure we didn't have sleepers. And we would have had to stop possibly overnight in Minneapolis when we got there. I don't remember any of that. I do faintly remember some of the train ride.

---

[Source: memoirs_main, Time: 2:54]
And there were—the two boys went with the boxcars of animals. And they tell of all the fun they had riding in those side door pullmans, if they call them, along with the animals, all the way up from Remsen, Iowa, up to Sioux Falls, Sioux City, and then up what they call the Sioux Line, up to Halbrite. And landed there on the 17th of March, after a winter of the greatest snowfall the prairie had ever seen. There was an estimated 36 inches of snow in Malapa that winter. However, we landed at Halbrite. And now this is where my memory starts to get in gear. We stayed in the hotel. And I have a faint recollection of us being in this hotel. This is Mother and all the children. And then the boys and Dad arrived up there. Well, I guess Dad was on the train with us. And the boys came up with the livestock, and they had to unload. And take it out to the farm. Now, when they got out there, everything was under snow. They tell that they had to dig into a snowbank to find the barn, a stable. And I'd like to see, you know they - just what went on. These guys coming from Iowa were, if they had six inches of snow there, it was a big snowfall. And here they come out with three foot of snow on the level.

---

[Source: memoirs_main, Time: 4:28]
We landed in a farm. Six miles north of Halbrite. As I remember it, the house stood there for years and years after. It was a two-bedroom house with a dining room, kitchen combined. Or a dining room, living room combined, and quite a large kitchen. And that house stood there for 20, 30 years after we lived in it for the first summer. And I don't have any recollection of the sleeping arrangements, where we all slept or anything. But there were 12 of us. But the youngest one wasn't born yet. But there were 12 of us and mother and dad lived in that house. And I can't figure out, we must have had solid mattresses on the floors in order to find a place to sleep. But first I remember the snow was all gone when I remembered that. I can remember the ride out there with all the deep snow. All you could see was snow. As far as the eye could see, everything was white. And then everything blanks out to me. The next thing I can remember is that I would have been four and a half years old at that time. And Liona was just past three, about 14 months younger than I was.

---

[Source: memoirs_main, Time: 6:03]
And the two of us walked across the mile where they were building the house on our regular farm where we were going to live. And I can remember just a faint recollection. I guess we sneaked away without authority or anything. We followed the trail, just a trail over there. We followed the trail over there. And the people in the house, they gave us cookies. And I remember prunes. And Liona ate so many prunes that she had that nickname for years after Prunes Achen. And then I have no recollection of us moving into that new house.

Lindy’s family left Iowa because a land agent had pointed out that the prairie in Canada was far cheaper—about $20‑$25 an acre compared with roughly $80 in Iowa—and that there was plenty of room to grow a new farm. Dad was willing to take the risk and move “up in that god‑forsaken country” when the family was already well established in a good corn‑growing community down in Iowa. [Source: memoirs_main, Time: 1:21]  

They packed up three boxcars of mostly animals and some machinery and set off by train. The boys rode in side‑door pull‑mans, while the rest of the family travelled in the passenger cars, probably stopping overnight in Minneapolis on the way. The journey itself is only faintly remembered, but the family knew they had to haul a lot of livestock across the raw prairie. [Source: memoirs_main, Time: 1:21]  

The train finally arrived at Halbrite on March 17 after a winter that saw an estimated 36 inches of snow in the area. The family stayed in a hotel while the boys and Dad unloaded the animals. The snow was three feet deep, and they had to dig into a snowbank to find the barn and stable. [Source: memoirs_main, Time: 2:54]  

They settled on a farm six miles north of Halbrite in a two‑bedroom house with a combined dining‑room/kitchen. The house was lived in for the first summer, and the family—twelve people, the youngest not yet born—sleeping on mattresses on the floor because there were no proper beds. The snow eventually melted, and the family remembered the long white landscape and the hardships of building a new life in the Canadian prairie. [Source: memoirs_main, Time: 4:28]  

Later, the children walked across the mile where the house was being built on their regular farm, sneaking away and receiving cookies and prunes from the people there. Liona earned the nickname “Prunes Achen” for her love of the fruit. [Source: memoirs_main, Time: 6:03]

results.query: Tell me about Lindy's education and school years.
results.context:
[Source: memoirs_main, Time: 8:24]
And then Crow Lake School opened up again. And we used to go around the road. We drove horses the three miles. And in the summer when they needed the horses in the field, we walked the three miles. And then at certain times of the year when it was dry enough, we'd cut straight across the fields. And that was just two miles going across that way. And we used to go a walk, you know the mosquitoes would be so bad that we'd run to get away from them. You'd run for a ways and then you'd slow down. And then when they'd congregate again around you, you'd run for another stretch - and that's the way you got your education, on foot to school and back. Went there. And when I was about ten years or twelve years old - No, I wasn't twelve then, maybe eleven. And we were driving. A single horse on a buggy. And four or five of us would pile in that one-seated buggy. Some would stand in the back, stand on the axle. And an old great big horse that - that drove back and forth. And then, as I said, in the summertime when they needed them in the fields, well, we would walk. Well, I kept up. That was my education, going to school. But I will say this much, when you're in grade, by the time you're in grade four, you knew the times table. Not just the four times.

---

[Source: memoirs_main, Time: 9:55]
You knew them right up to twelve times. You knew spelling. You knew geography. You've got a good grounding in education. There's no... And writing. They're very particular about your writing. How you learn to write. Make the letters, letter perfect, so to speak. And then I... Remember when I was about ten years old, mother and dad took a trip back to Iowa via train. For some reason, I was picked to go back with them. I don't know if I was cute or so mean they couldn't leave me at home or what it was, but I went to Iowa with them. I remember quite a bit about that trip, about being at the various uncles' places. And there were cousins there my age, and we used to have a lot of fun playing. It was in the fall of the year. And then dad and I came back. And mother stayed. For some reason, she stayed. And dad and I came back, and I still remember us being in Minneapolis. And he went down to a bar room and took me down with him. And there were all kinds of food. You could have all the food you wanted for free. A glass of whiskey at that time, I guess, was about 25 cents. And then as we went home that night, I remember we went down the street, and he said, Take a look down this window. We looked down a basement window, and here there were newspaper machines down there,

---

[Source: memoirs_main, Time: 11:26]
running all automated newspaper machines. And the papers were coming out of there just like nobody's business. Then I remember going home from Minneapolis, and dad had me singing. I could sing pretty good. Some of the old songs of the day. And of course I'd sing, and then everybody would hand me a dime - a dime! I had dimes, and I had porters on the train that were selling candies, oranges, and apples, and all sorts of good things. No soft drinks in those days. But I ate so much of this stuff I got sick, and I started puking. And that was the end of the singing. This train ride to Minneapolis home was about, oh, I would imagine, about a 24-hour train ride to Minneapolis, yes. It would be, oh, 24 hours to get from our place up to Minneapolis. I don't remember anything else then until 19... Oh, no, not any detail, except milking cows and doing chores, and you start doing that when you're eight years old on the farm. And at 11 years old, I rigged up a team of horses on a hay rack, and I was a straw monkey in the thrashing house. At 12 years old, I was out disking the land with horses while the boys, all the older ones, were busy with the thrashing. I couldn't remember that very well.

---

[Source: memoirs_main, Time: 12:56]
And you really had to work when you were on the farm. As soon as you were old enough to pull weeds, at five and six years old, you're out pulling weeds in the garden. And when I was ... 11, that would have been 1913, that's right, then Sister Aloysia was already a novice in the convent, at 14. And Sister Hilary and I both went to the convent that winter. Mary got an education, too. I was 11 years old. Well, I... Yeah, 11 years old, and I had to do my own laundry. You were on Saturdays, you did your own laundry, you washed your clothes, got a change of clothes, took a bath once a week, I don't remember what bathtubs are. I think we just took a bath. The boys had their own room down in the washroom, in the laundry room. We'd take a bath out of an ordinary wash tub. The food was plain and simple and you had to eat what was put on your plate, or have a darn good reason why you didn't eat it. ... because you didn't like it, that you were sick or something was the only reason you got away from eating the food that was put before you. I remember I was in grade five,

---

[Source: memoirs_main, Time: 14:26]
and at Easter time, we went home, and I didn't go back. I don't know why I didn't go back. I don't remember if Sister Hilary went back then or not. But I started school at home, and I wrote my grade five exam at home. Then I must have missed a year, or I must have failed a grade, because I went... I passed my grade six,

gemma3:12b.text:
Lindy attended Crow Lake School, which was about three miles from his home, and traveled there by horse or walking, depending on the season and need for the horses [Source: memoirs_main, Time: 8:24]. He and other children would sometimes pile four or five into a single-horse buggy [Source: memoirs_main, Time: 8:24]. Lindy received a solid education, learning times tables up to twelve, spelling, and geography [Source: memoirs_main, Time: 9:55]. Writing was emphasized, with a focus on perfect letter formation [Source: memoirs_main, Time: 9:55].

At age eleven, Lindy traveled with his parents to Iowa by train [Source: memoirs_main, Time: 9:55]. He later took a 24-hour train ride to Minneapolis with his father [Source: memoirs_main, Time: 11:26]. During that trip, he sang songs and was paid with dimes, but he got sick from eating too many treats [Source: memoirs_main, Time: 11:26].

Farm chores were a significant part of Lindy's childhood; he began pulling weeds at age five or six [Source: memoirs_main, Time: 12:56] and milking cows around age eight [Source: memoirs_main, Time: 11:26]. He worked as a "straw monkey" in the thrashing house at age eleven [Source: memoirs_main, Time: 11:26] and disked land at age twelve [Source: memoirs_main, Time: 11:26]. 

At age eleven (1913), his sister Aloysia was a novice in a convent [Source: memoirs_main, Time: 12:56]. Lindy and his sister Hilary both went to the convent that winter [Source: memoirs_main, Time: 12:56]. results.context:
[Source: memoirs_main, Time: 40:07]
I was getting thirty-five cents an hour, working nine hours a day, paying my own room and board, which you could get anyplace for a dollar a day. So I was making about what, uh 3 times 20 - making a little over $3 a day and paying $1 a day for room and board. I had - In the meantime, I had bought a car, a Model T Ford, for about $350. Good, reliable car. And then away I go, and I followed the outfit. They moved away from Worthington, where we had started, and one day the boss come along and said, we're moving across the state, down near Sioux City, Iowa. He said, "Do you want to go along with us?" Well, I said, "I will." Oh, in the meantime, he had come along one day. He said, "Look", he said, "I put in for a raise for you for 40 cents an hour. And they turned it down at the head office. I'm going to plug enough time that you'll get 40 cents an hour." He said, "I'll plug your time." He said, "I like the way you work." When to get back, well, I said, "Yeah, I'll go across to Iowa, but I don't think I should have to dig any more holes." I said, "I can dig more holes than anybody you've got around here. It's time I learned something else about the business." Well, he said, "You won't have to." He said, "Well, I'll start you out on other work that you get to familiarize yourself with the building of lines."

---

[Source: memoirs_main, Time: 41:43]
So when we got over to, it was Homer, Nebraska, just south of Sioux City, Iowa, where we were working. And there I started doing other things, framing cross arms and helping set poles and tamping poles and lining poles and putting on cross arms. And pretty soon we started stringing wire, and I knew a little bit about climbing poles because I'd fix telephones at home. So I started climbing poles, and that was the start of my career as a lineman. This takes us into about the first of March, 1927. And it was spring down there. Spring broke up early, and there was no snow there by the first of March. It was real mild weather. And then one Sunday, I took a notion to drive up to see my brother Lorie up at Sioux Falls, South Dakota, which is about 150 miles away. And when I get up there, I found him in a terrible shape. He had sciatica, and he had lost his wife the year before, and he had eight kids. The oldest girl was about 17. And the oldest boy was about 13.

---

[Source: memoirs_main, Time: 43:14]
So, oh, he was in a terrible way. He could hardly walk. Didn't know how he was going to get his crop in or anything. So I said, "I'll quit my job, and I'll come up and I'll put your crop in for you," I said. When I went back, told the foreman I was going to quit, and I said, "I like this work, but I don't like the life that's with it." I said, "You're sort of a, a transient wherever you go.", I said, "You're never in one place very long." And I said, "I think I better leave it right now and start doing something else." He said, "You'll leave it now, but you'll be back." He said, "I know you. You're the type. You'll be back." So I went back up to Sioux Falls and took over - took charge of my brother's farm. So I'll just rest for a moment now. Well, after a pause and taking a bit of a rest, I'll start in at my brother's farm. Now remember, I'm 25 years old. I'd run the home place for three years. So I was pretty well prepared to take charge of almost anything that come along.

---

[Source: memoirs_main, Time: 44:46]
I had never worked on a corn farm, and this was strictly corn, cattle, and hogs. So instead of driving a six or eight horse outfit here, you were driving a two or a four. And with his direction, I got everything done. I planted the corn and checked the corn when you planted you have a wire that you string along and it trips every 36 inches it trips the corn into the ground but you have to keep that lined up that you can cultivate it both ways. Everything went fine we got along good and had his crop all in the corn cultivated and everything going fine, and then July all of a sudden about the middle of July here comes a hail storm. And it just knocked everything to pieces. Well, he didn't know what to do. It knocked the old crop down. He had 40 acres of oats almost ready for the binder. Well, I said, "We'll call in the neighbors and mow her down, stack her up." So I called in the neighbors. We mowed down the 40 acres in a hurry and had it all stacked up and everything, and now I'm finished. In the meantime, I'd been - while I was in Iowa, my cousin from Nebraska, David City, Nebraska, said, when you're down in our part of the country, come and see us.

---

[Source: memoirs_main, Time: 46:34]
So I had been in touch with them, and they said harvest was coming on there. Why didn't I come down and help them harvest? So I did that. I went down, and they were right in the throes of cutting the grain. In those days, they still cut it with the binders, with horses and binders. And we thrashed. I was a stooker, setting up stooks. And when that was finished, well, we started then thrashing. The neighbors had an outfit, and I hauled bundles on that thrashing outfit. And we got all the crops thrashed. Well, there were some experiences on that that would make you smile if you knew what kind of a - an ornery bugger I was. The guy - he wouldn't move the machine, and I was pitching in the dust all day long. So I got mad in the afternoon, and I filled that machine so damn full of straw. I took him an hour to clean it out. And then he got mad at me. He gave me hell. I said, "If you knew anything about an outfit, I couldn't plug the machine." Well, he said, "If you know so much about it, let's see you set it that you can't plug it." So I said, "Get me a pliers and a wrench, and I'll set her." So I set it. that you couldn't plug it no how. The feeder would stop if it got coming in too fast. Well, he never said anything until after I left it came back to me that he said, "I guess that guy did know something about a thrashing outfit."

---

[Source: memoirs_main, Time: 48:12]
Well, we finished there and then I headed north I didn't know where I was going I stopped in at my brother's place in Sioux Falls and I had an uncle up near Aberdeen South Dakota so I I knew the oldest boy. I'd met him in Iowa. He was just my age. So I went up there, and when I got up there, well, it was just haying time. They were not in - well, they had been doing some harvesting. Anyway, I said I was looking for a job. I'd like to run the thrashing outfit. This is what I was after. So they turned me on to an outfit. It was the best thrasher in the country. So I drove up there, brave as hell, 25 years old, applied for the job. Oh, he said, I'm sorry, I just engaged a fellow this afternoon. But on Saturday, we're running through some oats to have horse feed for the thrashers. And come on up anyway. Well, I went up, and as it was, the other guy didn't turn up. So we started thrashing, and we hadn't gone ten minutes, I don't think, and all of a sudden, biffity-bang, and pieces started flying, and part of the concave, that's the part that does the thrashing in the front of the machine, had broke loose and gone into the blower, smashed up the blower and everything.
